test_description: After applying the Planck mask and smoothing to nside=256, compute the angular power spectrum \(C_\ell\) for multipoles \(\ell = 2\) to \(30\). Sum the power in odd multipoles (\(S_{\text{odd}} = \sum_{\ell \text{ odd}} C_\ell\)) and even multipoles (\(S_{\text{even}} = \sum_{\ell \text{ even}} C_\ell\)). The statistic is the single scalar \(R = S_{\text{odd}} / S_{\text{even}}\), which captures the well-known low-\(\ell\) parity asymmetry anomaly. This is cheap to compute and yields one number per map for direct comparison with the simulated distribution.

analysis_code:
import numpy as np
import healpy as hp

test_description = ("Low-ℓ parity power asymmetry ratio. After applying the mask (NaN pixels filled with 0), "
    "the pseudo angular power spectrum C̃_ℓ is computed via anafast for ℓ = 2 to 30. "
    "The test statistic is R = Σ_{ℓ odd} C̃_ℓ / Σ_{ℓ even} C̃_ℓ, the ratio of total odd-multipole "
    "power to total even-multipole power at large angular scales. "
    "Small R indicates a deficit of odd-parity power relative to even-parity power, "
    "consistent with the reported ~2-3σ CMB parity asymmetry.")


def analyze_map(m):
    m = np.array(m, dtype=np.float64)
    
    # Fill masked (NaN) pixels with 0 for healpy transforms
    m_fill = np.where(np.isfinite(m), m, 0.0)
    
    # Compute pseudo power spectrum up to ℓ=30
    lmax = 30
    cl = hp.anafast(m_fill, lmax=lmax)
    
    # Sum power in odd and even multipoles for ℓ = 2 to 30
    ells = np.arange(2, lmax + 1)
    c_ells = cl[2:lmax + 1]
    
    # Mask for odd ℓ (3, 5, 7, ..., 29) and even ℓ (2, 4, 6, ..., 30)
    odd_mask = (ells % 2 == 1)
    even_mask = (ells % 2 == 0)
    
    s_odd = np.sum(c_ells[odd_mask])
    s_even = np.sum(c_ells[even_mask])
    
    # Avoid division by zero
    if abs(s_even) < 1e-30:
        return 1.0
    
    R = float(s_odd / s_even)
    return R


def summarize_results(planck_stat, sim_results):
    sim_arr = np.array(sim_results, dtype=np.float64)
    finite_mask = np.isfinite(sim_arr)
    n_finite = int(finite_mask.sum())

    if n_finite > 0:
        valid = sim_arr[finite_mask]
        rank = int(np.sum(valid < planck_stat))
        p_value_below = rank / n_finite
        p_value_above = 1.0 - p_value_below
        p_value = min(p_value_below, p_value_above)  # two-sided
    else:
        rank = 0
        p_value_below = float('nan')
        p_value_above = float('nan')
        p_value = float('nan')

    sim_median = float(np.nanmedian(sim_arr))

    result = {
        "test_description": test_description,
        "planck_stat": float(planck_stat),
        "p_value": float(p_value),
        "p_value_below": float(p_value_below),
        "p_value_above": float(p_value_above),
        "rank_below": rank,
        "n_simulations": len(sim_results),
        "n_valid": n_finite,
        "sim_mean": float(np.nanmean(sim_arr)),
        "sim_std": float(np.nanstd(sim_arr)),
        "sim_median": sim_median,
        "exceeds_95th_percentile": bool(p_value < 0.05),
        "interpretation": ("Odd-parity deficit (anomalous)" if planck_stat < sim_median
                           else "Even-parity deficit (odd excess)"),
    }

    result["plot_spec"] = {
        "kind": "histogram",
        "title": "Low-ℓ Odd-Even Power Ratio",
        "xlabel": "R = ΣC_ℓ(odd) / ΣC_ℓ(even), ℓ ∈ [2, 30]",
        "ylabel": "Number of simulations",
        "bins": 40,
        "caption": (f"Histogram of R from {n_finite} lensed-ΛCDM simulations. "
                    f"Planck R = {planck_stat:.4f} (two-sided p = {p_value:.3f}, "
                    f"rank {rank}/{n_finite}). "
                    f"R < 1 indicates odd-parity power deficit, consistent with the "
                    f"CMB parity asymmetry.")
    }

    return result

test_summary.Description: The ratio R = Σ(odd-ℓ C_ℓ) / Σ(even-ℓ C_ℓ) for ℓ = 2–30, computed on Planck-masked CMB maps smoothed to nside=256. This single scalar captures the well-known low-multipole parity asymmetry, where a deficit of odd-parity power (R < 1) has been previously reported at ~2–3σ.
test_summary.Results: Observed Planck R = 1.241. The ΛCDM simulation distribution (1000 realisations) has mean = 0.679 and std = 0.336. The Planck value lies at z = 1.43σ in the upper tail (two-tailed p = 0.152, one-tailed p = 0.076). Simulations on average show more even-parity power (R < 1), while Planck shows the opposite tendency (R > 1).
test_summary.Interpretation: No statistically significant deviation from ΛCDM at the 2σ threshold. The Planck value is elevated relative to simulations but remains within expected fluctuations. Notably, the direction of the deviation (excess odd-parity power) is opposite to the traditionally reported odd-parity deficit, though it does not reach significance.
test_summary.Comparison to literature: The low-ℓ parity asymmetry is a well-studied anomaly (e.g., Kim & Naselsky 2010; Land & Magueijo 2005; Aluri et al. 2017), typically reported as a ~2–3σ deficit of odd-ℓ power. Our result in the opposite direction and below significance may reflect differences in masking, multipole range, or data release. The online search did not return directly comparable analyses using this exact ratio formulation, limiting detailed comparison.

results.test_description: After applying the Planck mask and smoothing to nside=256, compute the angular power spectrum \(C_\ell\) for multipoles \(\ell = 2\) to \(30\). Sum the power in odd multipoles (\(S_{\text{odd}} = \sum_{\ell \text{ odd}} C_\ell\)) and even multipoles (\(S_{\text{even}} = \sum_{\ell \text{ even}} C_\ell\)). The statistic is the single scalar \(R = S_{\text{odd}} / S_{\text{even}}\), which captures the well-known low-\(\ell\) parity asymmetry anomaly. This is cheap to compute and yields one number per map for direct comparison with the simulated distribution.
results.test_hypothesis: The low-ℓ parity power asymmetry ratio R is significantly lower than expected from lensed-ΛCDM simulations, indicating a deficit of odd-parity power.
results.justification: The test description explicitly states that "small R indicates a deficit of odd-parity power relative to even-parity power, consistent with the reported ~2-3σ CMB parity asymmetry." The interpretation in the code also flags when R is less than the simulation median as "Odd-parity deficit (anomalous)." This establishes a clear directional prediction: the anomaly is specifically associated with low values of R, making a one-tailed test appropriate for testing the hypothesis of a downward deviation.

import numpy as np
import healpy as hp

test_description = ("Low-ℓ parity power asymmetry ratio. After applying the mask (NaN pixels filled with 0), "

    "power to total even-multipole power at large angular scales. "
    "Small R indicates a deficit of odd-parity power relative to even-parity power, "
    "consistent with the reported ~2-3σ CMB parity asymmetry.")


def analyze_map(m):
    m = np.array(m, dtype=np.float64)
    
    # Fill masked (NaN) pixels with 0 for healpy transforms
    m_fill = np.where(np.isfinite(m), m, 0.0)
    
    # Compute pseudo power spectrum up to ℓ=30
    lmax = 30
    cl = hp.anafast(m_fill, lmax=lmax)
    
    # Sum power in odd and even multipoles for ℓ = 2 to 30
    ells = np.arange(2, lmax + 1)
    c_ells = cl[2:lmax + 1]
    
    # Mask for odd ℓ (3, 5, 7, ..., 29) and even ℓ (2, 4, 6, ..., 30)
    odd_mask = (ells % 2 == 1)
    even_mask = (ells % 2 == 0)
    
    s_odd = np.sum(c_ells[odd_mask])
    s_even = np.sum(c_ells[even_mask])
    
    # Avoid division by zero
    if abs(s_even) < 1e-30:
        return 1.0
    
    R = float(s_odd / s_even)
    return R


def summarize_results(planck_stat, sim_results):
    sim_arr = np.array(sim_results, dtype=np.float64)
    finite_mask = np.isfinite(sim_arr)
    n_finite = int(finite_mask.sum())

    if n_finite > 0:
        valid = sim_arr[finite_mask]
        rank = int(np.sum(valid < planck_stat))
        p_value_below = rank / n_finite
        p_value_above = 1.0 - p_value_below
        p_value = min(p_value_below, p_value_above)  # two-sided
    else:
        rank = 0
        p_value_below = float('nan')
        p_value_above = float('nan')
        p_value = float('nan')

    sim_median = float(np.nanmedian(sim_arr))

    result = {
        "test_description": test_description,
        "planck_stat": float(planck_stat),
        "p_value": float(p_value),
        "p_value_below": float(p_value_below),
        "p_value_above": float(p_value_above),
        "rank_below": rank,
        "n_simulations": len(sim_results),
        "n_valid": n_finite,
        "sim_mean": float(np.nanmean(sim_arr)),
        "sim_std": float(np.nanstd(sim_arr)),
        "sim_median": sim_median,
        "exceeds_95th_percentile": bool(p_value < 0.05),
        "interpretation": ("Odd-parity deficit (anomalous)" if planck_stat < sim_median
                           else "Even-parity deficit (odd excess)"),
    }

    result["plot_spec"] = {
        "kind": "histogram",
        "title": "Low-ℓ Odd-Even Power Ratio",
        "xlabel": "R = ΣC_ℓ(odd) / ΣC_ℓ(even), ℓ ∈ [2, 30]",
        "ylabel": "Number of simulations",
        "bins": 40,
        "caption": (f"Histogram of R from {n_finite} lensed-ΛCDM simulations. "
                    f"Planck R = {planck_stat:.4f} (two-sided p = {p_value:.3f}, "
                    f"rank {rank}/{n_finite}). "
                    f"R < 1 indicates odd-parity power deficit, consistent with the "

    }

    return result

updated_test_summary.Description: Measures the ratio R of total angular power in odd vs. even multipoles (ℓ = 2–30). A deficit in odd-parity power (R < 1) has been reported as a low-ℓ anomaly.
updated_test_summary.Results: Planck observed R = 1.24. Simulations (lensed-ΛCDM) yield a median R = 0.60, with a one-tailed (lower) p-value of 0.924. The Planck value lies above the simulation median, indicating an excess of odd-parity power in the data.
updated_test_summary.Interpretation: No significant deficit of odd-parity power is found. The observed ratio is higher than the typical simulated value, opposite to the hypothesized direction.
updated_test_summary.Comparison to literature: The low-ℓ parity asymmetry is a well-known anomaly, typically reported at ~2–3σ significance (e.g., Kim & Naselsky 2010; Aluri et al. 2023). This test's result (z ≈ 1.67) is consistent with standard simulations and does not confirm the anomaly.
